title: Simit Sesame Ring Bread

seo.title: Simit Recipe: Authentic Turkish Sesame Ring Bread
seo.metaDescription: Learn how to make the best authentic Simit recipe at home! This traditional Turkish sesame ring bread is perfect for breakfast or a snack. Get the easy step-by-step guide.
longTailKeywords: Simit bread recipe step by step
seo.h1: Authentic Simit Recipe: Turkish Sesame Ring Bread
seo.imageAlt: Freshly baked authentic Simit recipe, a traditional Turkish sesame ring bread, golden brown and delicious.
seo.imageCaption: Enjoy the irresistible aroma and taste of homemade Simit, a beloved Turkish sesame ring bread, perfect for any time of day.

description: Simit is a beloved Turkish ring-shaped bread, generously coated with sesame seeds, offering a delightful crunchy exterior and a soft, chewy interior. It is a staple of Turkish breakfasts and a popular street food, perfect with cheese, olives, or a cup of tea. This recipe brings the authentic taste of Istanbul's streets right to your kitchen.

instructions.title: Activate the Yeast
instructions.text: In a large mixing bowl, combine the warm water, granulated sugar, and active dry yeast. Stir gently until dissolved. Let the mixture sit for 5-10 minutes until it becomes foamy, indicating the yeast is active and ready.

instructions.title: Prepare the Dough
instructions.text: Add the all-purpose flour and salt to the activated yeast mixture. Mix with a sturdy spoon or your hands until a shaggy dough forms and most of the flour is incorporated. Turn the dough out onto a lightly floured clean surface.

instructions.title: Knead and First Rise
instructions.text: Knead the dough for 8-10 minutes until it becomes smooth, elastic, and no longer sticky. Lightly grease a clean bowl with olive oil, place the dough in it, turning once to coat. Cover the bowl with plastic wrap or a damp cloth and let it rise in a warm place for 60-90 minutes, or until it has doubled in size.

instructions.title: Shape the Simit
instructions.text: Gently punch down the risen dough to release the air. Divide the dough into 8 equal pieces. Roll each piece into a long rope, about 20-24 inches (50-60 cm) long. Take two ropes, twist them together, and then join the ends to form a ring. Repeat this process for all the remaining dough pieces.

instructions.title: Dip and Coat with Sesame Seeds
instructions.text: In a shallow dish, combine the grape molasses with 1/2 cup of water and mix well. In another shallow dish, spread out the toasted sesame seeds. Dip each simit ring first into the molasses mixture, ensuring it is fully coated, then immediately transfer it to the sesame seeds, pressing gently on both sides to adhere the seeds completely.

instructions.title: Second Rise and Bake
instructions.text: Place the coated simit rings on baking sheets lined with parchment paper, leaving enough space between them. Let them rest for a final 15-20 minutes. Meanwhile, preheat your oven to 400°F (200°C). Bake the simits for 15-20 minutes, or until they are beautifully golden brown and crispy.

instructions.title: Cool and Serve
instructions.text: Once baked, carefully transfer the simits to a wire rack to cool slightly. Simit is best enjoyed fresh and warm, but it can also be served at room temperature. Serve with traditional Turkish breakfast items like cheese, olives, and fresh tomatoes.

tips: For the most authentic taste, use Turkish grape molasses (üzüm pekmezi). If unavailable, date molasses or a mix of honey and water can be used as a substitute for the dip, though the flavor will differ slightly.
Ensure your water for the yeast is warm (105-115°F / 40-46°C), not hot, to activate the yeast properly without killing it. Cold water will make the rising process very slow.
Simit is best enjoyed fresh and warm. If you have leftovers, you can refresh them by lightly toasting them in a pan or oven. Store at room temperature in an airtight container for up to 2 days.
article: Simit Sesame Ring Bread is a beloved Turkish Baking dish enjoyed across Turkey. This authentic recipe brings traditional flavors to your kitchen.
